full_text:
Single-cell and spatial biomarkers for progression and responses to immunotherapy in cervical cancer

Naipunya Guruprasad
4th November 2025

Speaker notes:
Good afternoon everyone! Today, I’ll be continuing my presentation by sharing more about our progress in identifying single-cell and spatial biomarkers in cervical cancer.

BACKGROUND

Cervical cancer ranks as the fourth most prevalent cancer among women globally, 349,000 fatalities in 2022
Progression: 


CIN2 represents an intermediate stage of precancerous change in the cervical epithelium.
HPV-associated infections either regress or progress to higher-grade disease (CIN3 or invasive cancer), this mechanism remains poorly understood.

Bitsouni et al, From primary HPV infection to carcinoma in situ: A mathematical approach to cervical intraepithelial neoplasia. Studies in Applied Mathematics, 152(1), Article 12697. https://doi.org/10.1111/sapm.12697

Speaker notes:
To very briefly brush up on what Ella presented- cervical cancer is the fourth most common cancer among women, with around 3 hundred thousand deaths reported in 2022. It typically develops through a stepwise process: starting from HPV infection, progressing to precancerous lesions, and eventually cancer. The stage we focus on is CIN2, which represents an intermediate precancerous stage. This is the point where, lesions can either regress or progress to higher-grade diseases and we want to figure out the mechanism behind that.

AIMS

Cell phenotyping

Meta clustering

Spatial analysis

CIN2 project: Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia Grade 2 lesions

WHY DO SOME HPV INFECTIONS REGRESS TO NORMAL WHILE OTHERS PROGRESS?

Created using

Speaker notes:
our main goal in this project is to understand why some HPV infections regress to normal, while others progress.
From previous H&E work that ella mentioned, it was discovered that certain cell types and their spatial organization contribute to whether a lesion regresses or progresses. To explore this further, we first perform cell phenotyping to identify the major cell populations, then use meta-clustering to define more specific cell types, and finally carry out spatial analysis to see how these cells are arranged and interact within the tissue.

22-plex T-CyCIF antibody panel

FUNCTIONAL

LINEAGE

Cell Cycle & Proliferation

Immune Regulation & Checkpoints

Antigen Presentation & Signaling

Epithelial

Myeloid

Lymphoid

Stromal

Ki-67
p16INK4a
pRb

PD-1
PD-L1

PanCK
p63

pSTAT1
HLA-A
HLA-DPB1

Vimentin

Iba
CD163
CD11c

CD3D
CD4
CD8a
FOXP3
CD20
CD138
NKG2A

Speaker notes:
to carry out these aims, we used a 22-plex T-CyCIF antibody panel.
This panel includes a mix of lineage markers, which help us identify the main cell types in the tissue — like epithelial, lymphoid, myeloid, and stromal cells — and functional markers, which tell us about key biological processes such as cell proliferation, immune activation, and so on.
By combining both, we can not only identify the cell types, but also their cell states within the microenvironment — This is particularly important for spotting proliferating epithelial cells, that could be pre cancerous cells.

THE COHORT

Created using

Speaker notes:
Using this 22-plex T-CyCIF panel, we profiled a cohort of CIN2 lesions that were clinically followed over a two-year period. They were grouped into non-regressors, slow-regressors, and quick-regressors — depending on whether they stayed the same or regressed within one or two years.
We also included a few carcinoma samples taken at the beginning and end of the study, as progression references.
Altogether, These 65 samples were then stained and scanned for six cycles using Lola — thanks to Saun for running those and for bearing with me in the wet lab!

METHODS

〜 3M cells segmented so far

〜 6% of cells lost after QC so far

https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing

Speaker notes:
We then processed the images using our in-house preprocessing pipeline, which performs illumination correction, stitching using ASHLAR, segmentation using Stardist, quantification and quality control using CyLinter. So far, we’ve segmented around three million cells. 
After QC we saw about six percent cells filtered out.

65 samples

Pre-processing

Segmentation issues (2)
QC issues (6)
Additional samples (3) (carcinoma progressors)

Phenotyping - TRIBUS

51 samples

Manual gating (2)

Logic table correction (4)

45 samples

Analysis

Speaker notes:
So, for preprocessing, we started with 62 samples.
By the end of that step, we noticed that some had segmentation or QC issues, and we also decided to include three carcinoma samples that were later added into the cohort. These samples aren’t excluded — they’re just being troubleshooted this week.
That left us with 51 samples that were good post–preprocessing, which we then phenotyped using Tribus with quite a bit of trial and error. .
After that we found that some samples still need to be manually gated or need some tweaking with the logic table At the end of this, we now have 45 samples ready for analysis,

RESULTS: Cell phenotyping using TRIBUS

Stromal cells are dominant across most samples
Variation in epithelial, lymphoid, and myeloid cell proportions.

Speaker notes:
Looking at the preliminary results from the 45 samples, this plot shows the proportion of different cell types identified by Tribus across samples.
From the literature, we expected to see a strong presence of stromal cells and that’s exactly what we observe here in grey.
We also see clear variation in the proportions of epithelial, lymphoid, and myeloid cells, which is our main focus in these CIN2 lesions. This variation already hints at diversity within the cohort.
The dysplastic epithelial cells, shown in purple, are particularly interesting since these are associated with the precancerous regions.
These are still preliminary observations, but they give us a good foundation to start exploring how cell compositions might differ between regressor and non-regressor groups.

RESULTS:

UMAP Projections show low batch effects and good separation among phenotypes.

Speaker notes:
Now moving on to the UMAP projection. On the left, the cells are colored by cell type, and we can see clear separation between the main phenotypes — Intrestingly, we see some mixing between the normal and dysplastic epithelial clusters, which is expected in CIN2 lesions, since these regions represent cells going from normal to precancerous.
 On the right, the same projection is colored by sample ID which shows low batch effects.

NEXT STEPS

Finishing the preprocessing for all remaining images to complete our cohort for analysis. 

In parallel, exploring epithelial cell progression patterns across samples.

Using Recurrent Cell Neighborhoods (RCN) to study proportion of immune cells near neoplastic cells. 

Integrating our data with the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics results, which will help us link gene expression to cellular context.

p16INK4a (dysplastic epithelia) CD3D CD8 CD4

Speaker notes:
The next steps would be to finish preprocessing for all remaining images to complete the cohort for analysis.
In parallel, I’ll be exploring epithelial cell progression patterns across samples to identify common trends between regressor and non-regressor groups.
While going through the samples during quality control, it became even more evident and as reported in the literature, the proportion of immune cells near neoplastic epithelial cells could play an important role in spontaneous CIN2 regression. So We’ll then apply Recurrent Cell Neighborhood (RCN) analysis to study this relationship in more detail. Finally, as ella mentioned we’re integrating our data with GeoMx, where we’ve collected 243 regions of interest from 17 samples, capturing around 6,000 transcripts per region that will allow us to us to directly connect gene expression with cellular and spatial context..

Speaker notes:
Finally, I’d like to thank everyone who’s been part of this project since my time as a rotation student and also to the patients. I’d be happy to take any questions, or hear any ideas or suggestions you might have. Thank you!
